title: Service Profile Association Failures (Cisco UCS)
exclusions: Excludes Intersight-managed or Intersight-primary UCS domains where service-profile lifecycle, association retries, and template pushes are most truthfully read from the Intersight stream (for example `cisco:intersight:audit` or the Intersight API) instead of a classic UCSM-only poll into `cisco:ucs:inventory` and `cisco:ucs:audit` on this index. Excludes pure UCS Central federation topologies when profile association is orchestrated from Central in ways that your UCSM modular input set does not mirror. Excludes non-production org trees that run synthetic or intentionally broken service profiles to validate org-wide policy, naming, or vNIC templates—those are test beds, not paging targets. Excludes C-series in standalone CIMC mode with no UCSM registration, where `lsServer` DNs in Splunk are absent. Excludes legacy-only or IMM-hybrid domains where the same profile vocabulary labels differ in the GUI; tune field maps before you trust association semantics. This use case is UCS Manager–first; do not expect it to fire meaningfully for inventory that moved wholesale to a different data product without retuning the panel.
dataSources: `index=cisco_ucs` (or the index your `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` inputs write to) and, from the Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (Splunkbase 2731), the sourcetypes `cisco:ucs:inventory` (XML inventory MO snapshots; you filter `classId="lsServer"` for the Service Profile object), `cisco:ucs:audit` (UCSM audit-style lifecycle and operator actions on the same MO classes), and `cisco:ucs:faults` (class `faultInst` rows tied to managed-object DNs, including `lsServer` paths when UCSM files an association fault). Relevant managed-object classes are `lsServer` (profile identity, `assocState`, `configState`, `pnDn`, `srcTemplName`), with optional dependency context in `lsRequirement` and `lsBinding` in inventory when you extend the search. Old internal playbooks that cite a `cisco:ucs:config` sourcetype are not aligned with the shipped add-on: treat that as a documentation warning and use `cisco:ucs:inventory` and `cisco:ucs:audit` per Splunkbase 2731 field guidance.
app: `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` ([Splunkbase 2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731), v4.3.1 or newer, Splunk-supported on Splunk Enterprise 9.x, 10.x, and Splunk Cloud). The on-disk directory name and canonical app id use an underscore. Older local packages may still list a hyphen in the filename; that is a packaging quirk, not a second add-on. Install the TA on a Heavy Forwarder (or any node licensed for modular inputs) and on Search Heads for knowledge objects, saved searches, and accelerated reports.
spl:
index=cisco_ucs earliest=-4h@h
/* A — limit to inventory/audit: lsServer in inventory, full audit for lifecycle */
| search (sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" classId="lsServer") OR sourcetype="cisco:ucs:audit"
/* B — never score template objects as servers */
| eval type_clean=coalesce(type,"")
| where sourcetype!="cisco:ucs:inventory" OR !match(type_clean, "template$")
/* C — normalize field spellings the TA or XML can alternate */
| eval assoc=lower(coalesce(assocState,assoc_state,""))
| eval cfg=lower(coalesce(configState,config_state,""))
| eval sp_dn=if(sourcetype=="cisco:ucs:audit",coalesce(dn,objectDn,affectedObject,affectedDn,subject),coalesce(dn,""))
| eval sp_name=coalesce(name,"")
| eval pn=coalesce(pnDn,pn_dn,"")
| eval src_t=coalesce(srcTemplName,src_templ_name,"")
| eval res=lower(coalesce(result,outcome,status,opResult,operResult,""))
| eval ot=lower(coalesce(objType,objectType,className,""))
| eval op=lower(coalesce(action,operation,opType,op_type,opName,event,""))
| eval inv_fail=if(sourcetype=="cisco:ucs:inventory" AND classId="lsServer" AND (assoc IN ("failed","inaccessible") OR (assoc=="associated" AND (like(cfg,"failed%") OR cfg="failed-to-apply"))),1,0)
| eval audit_fail=if(sourcetype=="cisco:ucs:audit" AND match(lower(sp_dn),"/ls-") AND res IN ("failed","error","timeout","failure") AND (ot=="lsserver" OR ot=="") AND (like(op,"%associat%") OR like(op,"%decommission%") OR like(op,"%modif%") OR like(op,"%disassociat%")),1,0)
| where inv_fail=1 OR audit_fail=1
| stats max(_time) as last_signal
    first(inv_fail) as had_inv
    first(audit_fail) as had_audit
    values(sourcetype) as st_set
    latest(assoc) as assocState
    latest(cfg) as configState
    latest(sp_name) as name
    latest(pn) as pnDn
    latest(src_t) as srcTemplName
  by sp_dn
/* D — blast radius: peers sharing a template in this window; singletons are keyed per profile */
| eval src_key=if(srcTemplName!="",srcTemplName,"singleton_".sp_dn)
| eventstats count as peer_fail_count by src_key
| join type=left sp_dn
    [ search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" classId="faultInst" earliest=-4h@h
      | where lc!="cleared" OR isnull(lc)
      | rex field=dn "^(?<sp_key>org-root/.*?/ls-[^/]+)"
      | where isnotnull(sp_key)
      | stats latest(cause) as cause_enum, latest(severity) as fault_sev, max(_time) as fault_time by sp_key
      | rename sp_key as sp_dn ]
| fillnull value="" cause_enum
| eval severity_bucket=case(peer_fail_count>5 AND len(srcTemplName)>0,"template_blast",peer_fail_count>1 AND len(srcTemplName)>0,"template_cluster",assocState IN ("failed","inaccessible"),"isolated_lifecycle",1=1,"audit_or_apply")
| rename sp_dn as dn
| table last_signal, dn, assocState, configState, name, pnDn, srcTemplName, peer_fail_count, cause_enum, severity_bucket, had_inv, had_audit
| sort 0 - peer_fail_count - last_signal
description: Distinct from UC-19.1.2’s inventory compliance matrix, this use case merges `cisco:ucs:inventory` and `cisco:ucs:audit` for `lsServer` lifecycle failures: `assocState` `failed`/`inaccessible`, apply failures via `configState`, audit rows with error-class `result` on profile DNs, plus a `faultInst` join for `cause`. It drops template `type` rows, `coalesce`s name variants, and `eventstats` `peer_fail_count` by `srcTemplName` (TA Splunkbase 2731).
value: UC-19.1.2 is the scheduled posture story: it buckets every `lsServer` in `cisco:ucs:inventory` so you can run compliance and template campaigns. This use case is the live pipeline incident: a profile in `failed` or `inaccessible`, or a lifecycle action that returns `failed`, `error`, or `timeout` in audit, is a host that may not get the right identity to boot. The search adds a per-template `peer_fail_count` so a bad template push shows up as a wide blast, not a one-off. Without a shared Splunk view, on-call chases the UCSM Faults list and the service-profile FSM separately, misses how many `srcTemplName` peers fail together, and loses minutes in a P1. Keep UC-19.1.2 on the dashboard for programme health; page from this one when the association path itself is on fire, not for green-with-drift day-two cleanup.
implementation: Enable inventory, audit, and faults inputs in `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` so all three sourcetypes land in `cisco_ucs`, then save the primary `spl` as a 4-hour report, clone to a 15-minute real-time or short-window schedule for production paging, and throttle on `srcTemplName` plus `dn` so a single flapping FSM does not own the bridge. Add the companion `cause` histogram from `detailedImplementation` Step 2 for first-line triage.
visualization: Table by `peer_fail_count` and `last_signal` for the hottest templates; timechart of failing `assocState` rows; bar chart of `cause_enum` from `faultInst`; `srcTemplName` by `severity_bucket` heatmap; single value of profiles in `template_blast` or `template_cluster`. Place the row next to UC-19.1.2 posture panels to pivot from steady-state compliance to a live association drill.

references.title: Cisco UCS Manager Server Management Guide 4.2 — service profiles and lifecycle (GUI context)
Splunk documentation: coalesce, where, and eval for resilient field access

knownFalsePositives:
• A documented **BIOS token** or **secure-boot** policy mismatch on an association attempt can raise a short-lived `failed` or `inaccessible` read even when the later retry path is the approved remediation—confirm against the FSM in UCSM and the service ticket before a second page.
• An intentional **decommission** of a service profile can appear as a **failed** transition for one poll while UCSM tears storage and identities down in order; your change tool should list the `lsServer` as scheduled out, and the signal should be suppressed when the decommission is an approved, ticketed run.
• A **boot-policy** or **template** push that is *expected* to fail in a disaster-recovery or clone lab (for example, NIC placement that is invalid outside production) is not the same as an unexpected production fault—separate DNs in your suppression lookup so DR exercises do not look like a Los Angeles data-center outage.
• A central **LDAP** or **RBAC** outage on UCSM that blocks the audit or inventory modular input from completing gives you **zero rows** in Splunk, not a clean bill of health; treat sudden drops in `cisco:ucs:audit` volume as a data-fidelity incident before you believe an empty association panel.
• Planned **migrate-pool** or large pool moves can show a burst of `unassociating` then `associating` events in audit; pair with a change calendar and a minimum **dwell** on `failed` in inventory before a page, the same way you would not page on a single `establishing` poll in UC-19.1.2.
• A **faulty SFP** or **downstream link** that makes a server’s path **inaccessible** in inventory is a legitimate hardware and cabling problem, not an identity-policy error—route to the physical-link runbook, not the policy-runbook, when `cause` and the equipment fault stream already say optic or I/O.
• After a **UCSM major upgrade**, the human-readable `operation` labels in the audit stream can shift while the underlying `assocState` enums stay stable; if your alert is too tightly regex-bound to a verb, widen to `res IN (...)` and the `lsServer` DN, then re-baseline the verb list in a test domain.
• **Shorter audit retention** in Splunk than the window you use for `peer_fail_count` can make late-arriving `faultInst` events look like uncorrelated new failures; align retention, or narrow the `earliest` bound for both subsearches, before you accuse a template of a ghost blast radius.
• **Clock skew** between Splunk `_time` and the UCSM event stamp can reorder lifecycle stages in a 60-second micro-window; a human triaging in the GUI is authoritative for ordering; fix NTP on management paths if this happens often enough to break automation.
• **PowerTool** or **PowerShell** mass-updates to profiles can create a *correct* `peer_fail_count` spike for a `srcTemplName` that is also what your automation intended—add an automation-lookup key or maintenance tag so scripted waves do not page the same people twice.
• **Deduplication gaps** in fault indexing (same `faultInst` re-indexed) can make Splunk’s joined `cause` look noisier than the Faults pane; use `dedup` or `stats latest` in a forked test search before you change hardware against a false duplicate row.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This use case is not a second copy of UC-19.1.2. UC-19.1.2 classifies every `lsServer` in `cisco:ucs:inventory` into a compliance grid (compliant, drifted, pending-reboot, association-failure class, and so on) for a periodic posture review. UC-19.1.11 is for the incident where the association or policy-application lifecycle itself errors, times out, or lands a profile in `failed` or `inaccessible` right now, with a per-template blast-radius count. Run both: the first for programme health, the second for a 5–15 minute on-call runbook. Install Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs (Splunkbase 2731) on a Heavy Forwarder and Search Heads, enable the inventory, audit, and faults modular inputs, and use a read-only service account that can read `org-root`, the service-profile `ls-` objects, and the audit subtree per Cisco’s add-on install guide. Import the full UCSM TLS chain into the forwarder’s trust store, then validate `classId` and field names in Search before you alert. **Never** build this UC on a fictional `cisco:ucs:config` sourcetype; the shipped inventory path is `cisco:ucs:inventory` and lifecycle evidence is in `cisco:ucs:audit` plus `cisco:ucs:faults`.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**
(1) On the collection host, create or edit `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/local/inputs.conf` and enable the modular inputs the vendor documents for inventory poller, audit log (or audit-equivalent) pull, and faults; bind each to `index = cisco_ucs` (or your standard name) and keep the sourcetype the TA assigns (`cisco:ucs:inventory`, `cisco:ucs:audit`, `cisco:ucs:faults`). A representative pattern is to copy the stanza names from `default/inputs.conf` into `local/inputs.conf`, set `disabled = 0`, and fill `url = https://<ucsm-fqdn>`, the credential reference, and an interval appropriate to your license—association incidents usually tolerate one- to two-minute effective delay when inputs are healthy.
(2) After two full poll cycles, run discovery, in order: `index=cisco_ucs earliest=-1h | stats count by sourcetype` and confirm the three tags above are non-zero; `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-1h | stats count by classId | sort - count` to prove `lsServer` exists; and `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:audit" earliest=-1h | head 1 | fieldsummary` to see the spellings the TA uses for `result`, `action`, and `object` columns on your build.
(3) If your audit events lack `objType` but do carry `dn` under `org-root/.../ls-...`, the primary `spl` still works because the filter keys off `sourcetype` and a `/ls-` path; extend the `coalesce()` lists in a private clone if your site renames `result` to `opResult` or similar—do not invent a parallel `object_type` field for filtering.
(4) Document field variants in your runbook: the TA may expose `assocState` or `assoc_state`, `configState` or `config_state`, `pnDn` or `pn_dn`, `srcTemplName` or `src_templ_name`. The saved search in this UC uses `coalesce()` to absorb those spellings; when you add a new TA minor version, re-run a five-minute `head` sample and add any new names to the coalesce list before you file a false “fields missing” bug.

**Step 2 — Create the search, alert, and a companion “cause by enum” view**
(1) Paste the `spl` from this use case as a saved report named `uc_19_1_11_assoc_fail_4h` and keep Last 4 hours for engineering; schedule a 15-minute copy `uc_19_1_11_assoc_fail_15m` with the same SPL but a dashboard-friendly title for paging.
(2) Pipeline in plain language: the `search` line limits the universe to `lsServer` inventory rows and every audit event—if you do not have audit, remove the `OR` branch only after the business accepts inventory-only life-cycle, understanding you will miss short `failed` operations that never persist as a `failed` `assocState` in the next poll. The `!match(… template$ …)` line removes `initial-template` and `updating-template` objects from the inventory side. The `inv_fail` eval encodes: inventory-side red conditions—`failed` and `inaccessible` states, and “associated but apply failed” via `configState`—and `audit_fail` encodes: audit side red conditions—HTTP-like failure tokens in the normalized `result` field, profile DNs, and action verbs for associate, modify, decommission, or disassociate. The `stats` collapses to one row per `sp_dn` so a storm of audit retries does not duplicate the panel. The `eventstats` on `src_key` (template, or a synthetic singleton key) counts peers in the same failure window; that is the bad template versus one bad host story. The join to faults uses a `rex` to recover the `org-root/.../ls-ProfileName` prefix from a `faultInst` `dn` and pulls the last cause enum for the row.
(3) For alerts, add `| search severity_bucket IN ("template_blast","template_cluster","isolated_lifecycle")` in a clone if you do not want `audit_or_apply` pages during early tuning; pair every alert with a link to a saved UC-19.1.2 posture search filtered to the same `dn` to show whether the estate was already yellow before the lifecycle broke.

**Companion — top `cause` enums over 24h (operator clarity, not a substitute for the primary table)**

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" classId="faultInst" earliest=-24h@h
| where lc!="cleared" OR isnull(lc)
| rex field=dn "^org-root/.*?/ls-[^/]+"
| where match(lower(dn), "/ls-")
| stats count by cause, severity, code
| sort 0 - count
```

**Step 3 — Validate against Cisco UCS Manager**
(1) Take one `dn` from the result table, paste it into the Service Profile object in the UCSM GUI, and read assocState and configState; they should match the inventory line after a one- to two-minute poll offset that is normal for the TA.
(2) Open the Faults summary for the same profile and read the `cause` and severity; the joined `faultInst` row in Splunk should be the same family of facts, with the same one- to two-minute delay.
(3) If Splunk’s row count is higher than what UCSM still shows, you are likely re-indexing fault clears as new events, or a duplicated modular input; fix deduplication in props or the input, not the hardware.
(4) If Splunk’s count is lower than the GUI, the read-only user may lack rights to a subtree, audit may be disabled, or a firewall mid-flight dropped the poller: compare role scope in UCSM for the service account, then the forwarder’s `splunkd.log` for 401, 403, or TLS hand-shake text.

**Step 4 — Operationalize**
(1) Place the 4-hour and 15-minute panels on a row above the UC-19.1.2 compliance pie so a bridge can move from a green posture to a red lifecycle without changing dashboards. RACI: the UCS platform team owns the fix in UCSM; the SP owner (application or cluster) is consulted for maintenance impact; Splunk is the shared evidence system of record, not a replacement for the FSM in the vendor UI for legal root-cause sign-off.
(2) Throttle: use `throttle` fields `srcTemplName`+`dn`+`severity_bucket` and a 30- to 60-minute window so one template with ten bad hosts does not open ten primary incidents unless your process wants that.
(3) Suppress: wire a `maintenance` or change-calendar lookup for template waves so PowerShell, automation, and vendor bundle jobs do not re-page the on-call for expected blast radius.
(4) For audit evidence, export the 15-minute search as CSV to the same restricted share you already use for UC-19.1.2 weekly posture evidence; the filenames should include the UCSM cluster FQDN so a multi-site org never mixes DNs.

**Step 5 — Tuning and troubleshooting**
• No `cisco:ucs:audit` events even though the GUI has audit: input disabled, index routing typo, or RBAC. Re-enable the audit modular input, then re-run the sourcetype count.
• Inventory shows `lsServer` but all `inv_fail=0`: the failure is only on the FSM in flight—extend the window, or add a `join` to `cisco:ucs:events` in a private fork if your site indexes FSM there.
• `peer_fail_count` stuck at 1 for a wide template failure: the `srcTemplName` may be empty on child profiles; confirm `coalesce` includes every spelling, then fix naming CMDB, not the alert.
• `rex` join yields null `cause_enum` for real faults: the fault’s `dn` may not start at `org-root` the way your estate names orgs; fork the `rex` to `^(?<sp_key>org-root/.+?/ls-[^/]+)` versus a longer path—test with one fault’s `dn` in a five-line Search until it extracts.
• ClassId removed by a transform at index time: the join to faults will never fire; fix props before you add `classId` back into the search string.
• Summary indexing a pre-aggregated `assocState` is unsafe for this UC: you will miss the one poll that flips to `failed`; if you must accelerate, keep raw `lsServer` rows, not a daily roll-up, for association signals.
• UCSM HA failover the same second as a poll: one odd `assocState` read is not a mandate to replace a blade—wait a cycle.
• Intersight migration in progress: if `cisco_ucs` index volume drops, this UC’s green panel is a false calm; migrate the alert to the new data product, not the old silence.
• Blade swap weekend with ten profiles touched: you may get non-zero `peer_fail_count` in a test pool—scope suppression by org prefix.
• Firewall “SSL decrypt” middlebox that breaks the TA’s long poll: 502 or reset storms in `splunkd.log`; bypass or pin certificate until stable.
• C-series rack vs B-series path differences: both appear under an `org-root/.../ls-...` pattern when UCSM managed—if a rack is missing, the server is not in this UCSM domain, not a Splunk failure.
grandmaExplanation: We watch when the recipe that should land on a server during association or a big change fails mid-flight, and we name how many siblings share the same template so the right people fix a bad policy push versus one sick machine.
hardware: Cisco UCS B200 M4/M5/M6/M7 blade servers; C220, C240, and C480 M5/M6/M7 rack servers; X210c and X410c compute nodes in X9508 chassis; Fabric Interconnects 6248UP, 6296UP, 6332, 6332-16UP, 6454, 6536; IOMs 2204, 2208, 2304, 2408; X9108-IFM-25G and X9108-IFM-100G interface modules. Any UCSM-registered B-, C-, or X-series that carries an `org-root/.../ls-...` service profile is in scope for this search.
